notes.anchor: I should prefer cash to a valuation in kind
notes.note: Cicero contrasts numerato (payment in ready coin) with aestimatio (settlement by appraised property handed over at a fixed value). Under Caesar's debt legislation creditors could be forced to take land in lieu of cash, which Cicero wants to avoid here.

The sum stands in the Latin as HS DC, i.e. 600,000 sesterces, the standard Roman money of account. A first-class senatorial fortune ran into the millions, so this is a substantial but not extravagant sum toward the garden purchase.

notes.anchor: settle in full from Faberius or from someone who owes Faberius
notes.note: Faberius was Caesar's secretary (scriba); a large debt owed by or through him --- the recurring 'nomen Faberianum' of these letters --- is the fund Cicero hopes will finally pay for Tullia's shrine. Repraesentatio means paying the whole price down at once rather than by instalments.
